note_data.title: Nutrition
keyPoints: Nutrition is the process by which organisms obtain and utilize food materials for their growth, maintenance and energy requirements
Autotrophic nutrition — organisms synthesize their own food from simple inorganic substances (e.g., green plants)
Heterotrophic nutrition — organisms depend on other organisms for their food (e.g., animals, fungi)
Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts and requires sunlight, CO₂, water and chlorophyll to produce glucose and oxygen
Dark reaction (Calvin cycle) occurs in stroma while light reaction occurs in thylakoids of chloroplasts
Limiting factors of photosynthesis include light intensity, CO₂ concentration and temperature
Parasitic nutrition involves obtaining food from living host organisms (e.g., tapeworm, Plasmodium)
Saprophytic nutrition involves obtaining food from dead organic matter (e.g., mushrooms, bacteria)
Symbiotic nutrition involves mutual benefit between two organisms (e.g., lichens, nitrogen-fixing bacteria in legumes)

definitions.definition: The process by which organisms obtain and utilize food materials for their growth, maintenance and energy requirements
The mode of nutrition in which organisms synthesize their own food from simple inorganic substances
The process by which green plants synthesize glucose from carbon dioxide and water in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll
The mode of nutrition in which organisms obtain their food from living host organisms
The mode of nutrition in which organisms obtain their food from dead and decaying organic matter

importantQuestions.question: Differentiate between autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition.
importantQuestions.answer: Autotrophic nutrition: Organisms synthesize their own food from simple inorganic substances. Heterotrophic nutrition: Organisms depend on other organisms for their food.

importantQuestions.question: Describe the process of photosynthesis with its equation and factors affecting it.
importantQuestions.answer: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants synthesize glucose from CO₂ and water using sunlight and chlorophyll. Equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + sunlight → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. It occurs in two stages: Light reaction (in thylakoids) and Dark reaction (in stroma). Limiting factors include light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature, and chlorophyll content. This process is vital for oxygen production and food chains in Pakistan's agricultural areas like Sindh province.

commonMistakes: Students confuse raw materials (CO₂, H₂O) with products (glucose, O₂) in photosynthesis equation
Writing light reaction in stroma instead of thylakoids
Confusing parasitic and saprophytic nutrition examples
Not mentioning chlorophyll as essential requirement for photosynthesis
Forgetting to balance the photosynthesis equation properly
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Always write the complete balanced equation of photosynthesis — it appears every year
Memorize exact definitions as they appear in STBB textbook
Practice drawing and labeling chloroplast structure — frequently asked in diagrams
Learn examples of each type of nutrition with Pakistani context
note_data.keyDiagram: Structure of chloroplast showing outer membrane, inner membrane, stroma, thylakoids, and grana. Label parts where light and dark reactions occur. This diagram appears frequently in BSEK exams.
